beats.narration_text: Bula, Liam. This is Liam, in for Bear. Doubling the clock raised the current draw seventy-five percent — and the battery lasted longer.

props.command: How does running my MCU faster at higher current actually save battery energy?
output: Energy = current × time. Taller but narrower pulse beats a lower but longer one — then sleep wins.

beats.narration_text: From the book: two overlaid current traces. The sixty-four megahertz clock draws more while active but finishes in less than half the time — lower energy total. Race to sleep.

Slow clock: 80 megahertz, 8 milliamps active current, 22 milliseconds of inference time. The shaded area under that pulse is energy: 8 milliamps times 22 milliseconds equals 0.176 milliamp-seconds. Low current, wide pulse, large area.

Fast clock: 160 megahertz, 14 milliamps active — 75 percent more current. But inference completes in 11 milliseconds: half the time. Energy: 14 times 11 equals 0.154 milliamp-seconds. The pulse is taller and narrower, and the shaded area is 12 percent smaller. Faster wins.

Here is why it works. The fast clock finishes at 11 milliseconds and drops immediately to sleep current — 5 microamps, three orders of magnitude lower. The slow clock is still active at 11 milliseconds, burning 8 milliamps for another 11 milliseconds before it can sleep. Race to sleep: finish fast, go deep, save the area under the curve.

Your turn. Paste this into Claude: My MCU runs at clock-mhz megahertz, active current active-mA milliamps, sleep sleep-uA microamps. Inference takes inference-ms milliseconds. I can double the clock to fast-clock megahertz, which raises active current to fast-mA milliamps but halves inference time. For inference frequencies of 1 per second to 10 per second: compute energy per inference for both clocks, find the crossover duty cycle where the faster clock starts losing, and plot the battery life for each. Swap in your chip specs and inference numbers.

props.command: My MCU runs at [clock_mhz] MHz, active current [active_mA] mA, sleep [sleep_uA] µA. Inference takes [inference_ms] ms. I can double the clock to [fast_clock_mhz] MHz, which raises active current to [fast_mA] mA but halves inference time. For inference frequencies of 1/s to 10/s: compute energy per inference for both clocks, find the crossover duty cycle where the faster clock starts losing, and plot the battery life for each.

props.title: Run Faster to Save Power
props.subline: embedded ai · liam, in for bear
